verificationNote: Sector-specific requirements are constructed from IEC 62443, IEC 61511 (functional safety), and NACSA Act 854 obligations for the Malaysian oil and gas sector. The TRITON/TRISIS attack (Petro Rabigh, 2017) specifically targeted Safety Instrumented Systems in the petrochemical sector and directly informs this sector's threat model. These requirements have not been endorsed by PETRONAS, DOSH, or NACSA.
nacsaSectorLead: Ministry of Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability / Department of Occupational Safety and Health (DOSH) for SIS
applicableStandards: IEC 61511 (Functional Safety — Safety Instrumented Systems — SIL)
sectorOtCharacteristics: Safety Instrumented Systems are life-safety and environmental protection devices — SL 4 mandatory
TRITON/TRISIS (2017) was the first malware specifically designed to attack SIS (Triconex Safety Controllers)
Large geographic footprint — upstream production, midstream pipelines, downstream refineries, LNG terminals
High process consequence of control system failure — fires, explosions, environmental release
IEC 61511 compliance is a DOSH requirement for SIS in Malaysian industrial facilities
Petronas upstream: safety-critical systems are NCII assets

requirements.name: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) cyber security
requirements.description: SIS (Triconex, Hima, Yokogawa ProSafe, Emerson DeltaV SIS) are the last line of defence against process safety incidents. The TRITON attack demonstrated that sophisticated adversaries specifically target SIS to disable safety protection before launching a more destructive attack on the DCS. SIS must be treated as the highest-security OT asset — SL 4 target, network isolated from DCS, with strict physical access controls.

basis: DOSH — Occupational Safety and Health Act 1994 (Act 514)
legal.summary: IEC 61511 (DOSH-required for SIS) requires SIS management of change procedures that encompass cybersecurity. IEC 62443 SL 4 is required for all SIS per the NACSA cross-reference. TRITON-class attacks represent a state-sponsored threat that specifically targets safety systems to enable physical destruction.
legal.owner: Safety Instrumented Systems Engineer / OT Security Manager / Safety Manager

technical.summary: SIS completely isolated from DCS and all other networks. SIS engineering workstation air-gapped or on dedicated isolated segment. No remote access to SIS under any circumstances. Physical access with two-person rule. SIS program integrity verified via checksum after every maintenance event.
actions: SIS controller network completely isolated from DCS — separate physical cables, separate physical switches; no logical path from DCS to SIS
SIS engineering workstation (EWS): air-gapped from all networks; media handling requires two-person protocol and scanning workstation
No remote access to SIS under any circumstances — this is an absolute requirement regardless of operational convenience
Physical access to SIS controllers and EWS: locked SIS room separate from DCS control room; access requires two persons from approved list; physical log maintained
SIS program integrity: record SHA-256 hash (or vendor-provided checksum) of each SIS program after commissioning and after every approved change; verify before each maintenance event
SIS key switch: SIS controllers must be in RUN mode (hardware key switch) during operations — PROGRAM mode only during approved maintenance with two-person awareness
Security assessment of SIS: assess SIS system against IEC 62443-3-3 SL 4 requirements — assess current SL-A and document gaps; use only qualified OT security assessors familiar with SIS cybersecurity

governance.summary: SIS cybersecurity is part of the IEC 61511 SIS management of change procedure. All SIS changes require SIS engineer + safety manager approval. No SIS cybersecurity exceptions without C-suite risk acceptance.
actions: All SIS program changes go through formal management of change with SIS engineer + safety manager approval
SIS program checksum register maintained as controlled document — physical copy in SIS room
Annual SIS security review against IEC 62443-3-3 SL 4 requirements
SIS cyber security incident (any unauthorised access or program modification) triggers immediate plant safety review

evidenceItems: SIS network isolation architecture (physical separation documented)
SIS EWS configuration (air-gapped or isolated segment)
SIS management of change records (IEC 61511 compliant)
SIS key switch policy and verification records
SIS security assessment report (SL 4 gap analysis)

slMapping.sl4: All controls above are SL 4 requirements. SL 4 is mandatory for all SIS regardless of NCII designation. SL 3 is not acceptable for SIS — the TRITON attack demonstrated SL 3-calibre adversaries specifically target SIS.

requirements.name: Pipeline SCADA security and leak detection
requirements.description: Pipeline SCADA provides remote monitoring and control of high-pressure oil, gas, and LNG pipelines. Security requirements include: protection of SCADA from WAN attack, integrity of flow and pressure sensor data used for leak detection, and protection of emergency shutdown commands.

legal.summary: Pipeline SCADA is NCII OT. API 1164 (widely adopted reference) provides pipeline SCADA security guidance aligned with IEC 62443. NACSA s18 and s21 apply.
legal.owner: Pipeline Control Engineer / OT Security / HSSE

technical.summary: Pipeline SCADA: encrypted WAN communications, integrity-verified flow/pressure data, CPM (computational pipeline monitoring) for leak detection cross-validated against SCADA data, remote RTU access via jump server.
actions: Pipeline WAN: MPLS or encrypted VPN for all RTU/compressor station to SCADA communications — no cleartext DNP3/Modbus over internet
RTU/compressor station remote access: jump server with MFA; all sessions recorded; time-limited vendor sessions
Flow and pressure sensor data: SCADA validation logic detects sensor outliers; CPM leak detection uses independent sensor data path where possible
Emergency valve closure/ESD commands: command authentication and confirmation required from SCADA operator — not single-click
OT network monitoring on pipeline SCADA WAN — detect anomalous control commands (unexpected valve operations, ESD activations)
Compressor station physical security: remote sites with camera/alarm; perimeter fencing; cellular modem isolated from control network

governance.summary: Pipeline SCADA security reviewed annually and after any significant incident or near-miss.
actions: Annual pipeline SCADA security review
CPM vs SCADA data correlation reviewed monthly
Emergency command procedure tested in annual exercise

evidenceItems: OT monitoring coverage of pipeline SCADA

slMapping.sl2: Encrypted WAN; jump server for remote access; physical security at compressor stations
slMapping.sl3: OT monitoring on pipeline SCADA WAN; sensor validation; emergency command confirmation; PAM with session recording
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic command authentication for pipeline ESD; independent CPM data path; continuous integrity monitoring of RTU configurations

requirements.name: Process plant DCS security (refinery, petrochemical)
requirements.description: DCS (Distributed Control System) in refineries and petrochemical plants controls complex, high-energy processes. Compromise could allow setpoint manipulation causing product quality failures, equipment damage, or process safety incidents. DCS is the primary target after SIS has been disabled in a TRITON-class attack scenario.

legal.summary: Process plant DCS is NCII OT. NACSA s18 and s21 apply. DCS security is also implicitly required under DOSH Act 514 occupational safety obligations for high-hazard facilities.
legal.owner: DCS Engineer / OT Security Manager / Process Safety Engineer

technical.summary: DCS network fully segregated from business network; IDMZ historian for data export; application whitelisting on DCS servers; OT network monitoring on DCS LAN; vendor access via PAM-controlled jump server.
actions: DCS control network physically segregated from business network; IDMZ with historian for data export
DCS servers (HMI, application server): application whitelisting; no internet access; OT-specific hardening applied
DCS vendor remote access: dedicated jump server with MFA; all sessions recorded; session duration limited to active work only; no persistent VPN
OT network monitoring on DCS LAN: alert on new device, unexpected communication patterns, off-hours engineering access, DCS component restarts
Process setpoint changes: log all setpoint changes in DCS audit log; alert on out-of-window changes
DCS patch management: coordinate with vendor on patching cadence; test patches in non-production before applying; accept temporary risk before high-risk patches

governance.summary: DCS security part of plant cybersecurity management system. Reviewed annually.
actions: Annual DCS SL assessment (SL 3 target for primary process units)
DCS firewall changes via change management — OT security manager approval required
DCS patch schedule agreed with vendor and plant management annually

evidenceItems: DCS network architecture (segregation from business network)
PAM records for vendor DCS access
OT monitoring coverage of DCS LAN

slMapping.sl2: DCS segregated; IDMZ historian; vendor access via jump server
slMapping.sl3: OT monitoring on DCS LAN; application whitelisting; MFA on all remote access; setpoint change alerting
slMapping.sl4: All SL 3 controls plus: dual-person approval for safety-relevant setpoint changes; cryptographic integrity of DCS configuration; SIS isolation verified continuously

sectorSpecificThreats.threat: DCS setpoint manipulation for process damage
